Home › Calculators › Salary Gross-Up Calculator

💰 Salary Gross-Up Calculator

Know how much you need to take home, and want to find the salary that delivers it? Enter your target net income and your deduction rate to work out the gross salary you'd need.

Working backwards from take-home

Job offers and salaries are quoted in gross (pre-tax) terms, but you live on net (take-home) pay. So a natural question when planning your finances or evaluating an offer is the reverse of the usual one: "I need to take home $5,000 a month — what salary does that require?" This calculator answers it by "grossing up" your target net pay.

How the gross-up works

If deductions take a fraction d of your gross pay, then your take-home is gross × (1 − d). Flip it to solve for gross: gross = net ÷ (1 − d). If you want $5,000 net a month and 25% goes to tax and deductions, you need $5,000 ÷ 0.75 ≈ $6,667 gross a month, or about $80,000 a year. Notice the gross-up isn't simply "add 25%" — because the deduction applies to the larger gross figure, you need to divide, not multiply. (Adding 25% to $5,000 would give only $6,250, which nets less than you wanted.)

Where this is useful

  • Setting a salary target. If your budget needs a certain take-home, this tells you the salary to aim or negotiate for.
  • Evaluating job offers or moves. Compare what gross salary a new role needs to match your current take-home, especially across places with different tax rates.
  • Freelance and contract pricing. If you need a certain net income, grossing up shows what you must bill before tax and expenses — pair it with the [freelance rate calculator](/calculators/freelance-hourly-rate-calculator/).

Getting the deduction rate right

The accuracy depends on your deduction rate. Use your effective rate — total deductions divided by gross — not your top tax bracket, which overstates it (see [marginal vs effective tax rate](/articles/marginal-vs-effective-tax-rate/)). Your last payslip or tax return gives the real figure. Remember tax is usually progressive, so a much higher target may face a higher effective rate than your current one. Use the standard [take-home pay calculator](/calculators/take-home-pay-calculator/) to check the net a given gross actually produces.

Frequently asked questions

How do I calculate the salary I need for a target take-home?

Divide your target net pay by (1 minus your deduction rate). For $5,000 net a month at 25% deductions: $5,000 ÷ 0.75 ≈ $6,667 gross a month. Dividing, not adding a percentage, gives the correct figure.

Why can't I just add my tax rate to my target?

Because the deduction applies to the larger gross amount, not your net target. Adding 25% to a net figure under-shoots; you must divide by (1 − rate) to gross up correctly.

What deduction rate should I use?

Use your effective rate — total tax and deductions as a percentage of gross pay — from a recent payslip or tax return, not your top marginal bracket, which would overstate the deductions.

Further reading

"Understanding Your Paycheck: Where Your Money Actually Goes" A line-by-line guide to your pay stub — gross vs net, taxes, and deductions — so you know exactly why your take-home is less than your salary. "Marginal vs Effective Tax Rate: The Misunderstanding That Costs People Real Money" Why "being in the 24% bracket" doesn't mean losing 24% of your income, how progressive brackets actually work, and the raise myth debunked with numbers. "The 50/30/20 Rule in Real Life: Where It Works, Where It Breaks, How to Fix It" An honest field guide to the most famous budgeting rule — including what to do when rent alone eats 45% of your income. "How Much Should You Spend on a Car? Rules of Thumb That Actually Work" Practical guidelines for a sensible car budget — the 20/4/10 rule, why total cost dwarfs the sticker price, and how a car quietly drains wealth.

This calculator is for educational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. Results are estimates based on the inputs and assumptions shown.
